behaviors.description: The traveling-salesman heuristic produces a route that is no more than 1.5x the length of the optimal route for inputs up to 20 stops.
behaviors.justification: The TSP heuristic is a pure deterministic algorithm with no external dependencies or I/O. Real failure—producing suboptimal routes—can be caught by running the heuristic on known coordinate sets and comparing its tour distance against the true optimal (computed via brute-force enumeration or benchmarks). This requires only in-process computation.
behaviors.test_description: Create 5+ test cases with small coordinate sets (3–20 points) where optimal tour distance is known or computable via brute-force. For each, run the heuristic, compute its tour length, and assert heuristic_distance ≤ 1.5 × optimal_distance. Include test cases: collinear points, clustered points, uniformly distributed grid, and boundary cases (3 and 20 stops).

behaviors.description: The delivery time window estimates are computed correctly from the distance between consecutive stops and the configured average speed for the road type.
behaviors.justification: This is pure arithmetic: time = distance / speed, with configurable speeds per road type. Fully deterministic with no external dependencies or I/O. Real failures (incorrect formula, unit mismatch, wrong speed lookup) are caught by parametrized tests verifying calculation against known input/output pairs.
behaviors.test_description: Use parametrized tests covering multiple scenarios: (1) standard case: 10 miles at 30 mph city speed → 20 minutes; (2) highway case: 30 miles at 60 mph → 30 minutes; (3) fractional distances: 0.1 miles at 10 mph → correct fractional time; (4) boundary: 0 distance → 0 time. Test each road type (city, highway, residential) and verify time = distance / configured_speed_for_type.

behaviors.description: The shift-hours validator rejects a route whose total estimated time exceeds the driver's remaining shift hours, returning the excess amount.
behaviors.justification: This is deterministic comparison logic with no external dependencies: if total_time > remaining_shift_hours, reject and compute excess correctly. Real failures (wrong comparison operator, incorrect excess calculation) are caught by pure logic tests with known time pairs.
behaviors.test_description: Create test cases covering: (1) route within shift: total=4 hrs, remaining=6 hrs → accept, excess=0; (2) route exceeds shift: total=8 hrs, remaining=6 hrs → reject, excess=2 hrs; (3) exact boundary: total=6 hrs, remaining=6 hrs → accept (specify behavior); (4) near-boundary: total=6.0001 hrs, remaining=6 hrs → reject with correct excess ≈0.0001 hrs; (5) edge case: 0 remaining shift → reject any non-zero route. Verify both accept/reject decision and excess amount.

behaviors.description: When one address in the list fails geocoding (API returns no results), the service skips that stop and optimizes the remaining addresses, reporting which addresses were skipped.
behaviors.justification: This behavior depends on an external geocoding API and the service's handling of its failure response. While the error-handling logic is deterministic, catching the real failure—'does the service continue correctly when geocoding returns no results?'—requires mocking the external dependency at the integration boundary. This tests the service's contract with the geocoding API; pure in-process code cannot generate the failure scenario without dependency injection and mocking.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the geocoding service to return empty results for one address ('invalid address') but return valid coordinates for others. Call the route optimization service with [valid_addr_1, invalid_addr, valid_addr_2]. Assert: (1) the service returns an optimization result excluding the invalid address; (2) the route includes only the two valid addresses in optimized order; (3) the response reports skipped addresses, listing 'invalid_addr' with geocoding failure reason; (4) no exception is raised; (5) delivery items for the skipped address are not lost (either returned separately or merged into a report).

behaviors.description: Duplicate addresses in the input list are consolidated into a single stop with combined delivery items, not visited twice.
behaviors.justification: This is pure data processing logic: detecting duplicate addresses and merging their delivery items. Fully deterministic with no external dependencies or I/O. Real failures (duplicates undetected, items lost, address visited twice) are caught by in-process tests verifying consolidation correctness.
behaviors.test_description: Create test cases covering: (1) single duplicate: [addr_A(item1), addr_A(item2)] → [addr_A(item1, item2)]; (2) triple: [addr_X(item1), addr_Y(item2), addr_X(item3)] → [addr_X(item1, item3), addr_Y(item2)]; (3) no duplicates: [addr_A, addr_B, addr_C] → unchanged; (4) matching strategy: clarify whether matching is exact string, normalized string, or coordinate-based; test accordingly; (5) empty item lists: verify consolidation handles addresses with no items; (6) order preservation: verify consolidation preserves logical structure. Verify final route visits each unique address exactly once and all items are preserved.
